action.content:
Expand the riddle into a story with a funny tone:

What can you catch but not throw?
A cold

Use * to highlight at least 2 sections in your text. For example: *this is a highlighted text section*.
action.label: context

action.content:
Barnaby was a man who took the phrase "catch a cold" quite literally. While everyone else treated the common cold as a metaphorical sneeze of bad luck, Barnaby treated it like a baseball game. He spent his entire winter wardrobe collection in a desperate, futile attempt to *catch a cold before the flu season even had a chance to warm up*.

He stood in the middle of his living room, wearing nothing but a single sock and a very confused expression, waiting for a tiny, invisible baseball to pitch toward his nose. "Come on!" he shouted at the empty air, doing a little shimmy dance. "I'm ready! I've got my mitt on! Well, I don't actually have a mitt, but my imagination is doing a great job!"

His neighbor, Mrs. Gable, walked by with her shopping bags, looking at him with the pity one usually reserves for a dog chasing its own tail. "Barnaby," she called out, "you can't just stand there waiting for a virus to throw itself at you. You have to go out into the cold air!"

Barnaby paused, his socked foot hovering in mid-air. "But Mrs. Gable, that's the whole point! If I go outside, I might catch a *real* cold, and then I can't throw it back at the pitcher! It's a foul ball situation!"

Mrs. Gable sighed, shaking her head as she walked away. She didn't understand the logic, but she did understand the rules of the game. She knew that if you went out into the freezing wind, you wouldn't be catching a ball; you'd be *catching a cold that would make you sneeze so hard you'd accidentally launch yourself out the window*.

Barnaby, however, remained steadfast in his strategy. He decided to try a new tactic: he would sit by the open window and just stare at the frost forming on the glass. "You're coming," he whispered to the ice crystals. "I know you're coming. I can feel the chill in my bones. It's like a warm-up drill."

Sure enough, within an hour, Barnaby's nose began to run. But instead of blowing it into a tissue, he tried to *throw the snot back at the window*, hoping to score a home run. Unfortunately, he missed, and the snot just landed on his chin, looking like a very unflattering mustache made of clear gelatin.

By the time the doctor arrived, Barnaby was lying on the couch, shivering violently while holding a baseball glove. "Doc," he wheezed, "I think I finally caught it. But I'm worried I threw it too hard. Do you think I can still pitch next week?"

The doctor looked at the man with the running nose and the baseball glove, then gently placed a hand on Barnaby's forehead. "Barnaby," the doctor said with a straight face, "you can't throw a cold. And honestly, you probably shouldn't be trying to catch one with a glove either. Just rest, drink some tea, and let your immune system do the throwing for you."

Barnaby nodded slowly, finally realizing that while he could catch a cold, the only thing he could throw was a very loud, very embarrassing sneeze. "Right," he muttered, pulling the blanket up to his chin. "No throwing. Just... catching. Got it."

And so, Barnaby learned his lesson the hard way: some things in life are meant to be endured, not played like a sport. Though, he did keep the glove, just in case he ever needed to catch a cold again.
action.label: response
